inference_unit.notations: - $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature - $P$: pressure - $M$: atomic mass - $\alpha$: isotope effect coefficient - $\xi$: coherence length of the superconducting order parameter - $\phi_{0}$: magnetic flux quantum - $H_{c2}$: upper critical field - hcp: hexagonal close-packed crystal structure - fcc: face-centred cubic crystal structure - bcc: body-centred cubic crystal structure - $P6_{3}/mmc$: space group identifier for a hexagonal crystal system - $Im\bar{3}m$: space group identifier for a body-centred cubic crystal system

premises.premise: Theoretical calculations predict the existence of a hexagonal close-packed (hcp) yttrium hydride phase with the composition $YH_{9}$ and space group $P6_{3}/mmc$, which is predicted to be stable at high pressures such as 200 GPa. [22, 23, 24]

Theoretical calculations predict a face-centred cubic (fcc) yttrium hydride phase with the composition $YH_{10}$ that possesses a superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ estimated to be 303 K at a pressure of 400 GPa or between 305 K and 326 K at a pressure of 250 GPa. [22, 23]
The Vinet equation of state is a mathematical model used to approximate the relationship between the volume of a crystal lattice and the applied external pressure in solid materials. [30]

The isotope effect coefficient $\alpha$ in a superconductor is defined by the relation $\alpha = -\frac{d\ln T_{c}}{d\ln M}$, where $T_{c}$ is the superconducting critical temperature and $M$ is the atomic mass of the isotope.

The coherence length $\xi$ of the superconducting order parameter is related to the upper critical field $H_{c2}$ by the expression $\xi = \sqrt{\frac{\phi_{0}}{3\pi^{2}H_{c2}}}$, where $\phi_{0}$ is the magnetic flux quantum.

The temperature dependence of the superconducting upper critical field $H_{c2}$ can be approximated using the Ginzburg–Landau (GL) equation for the temperature regime near the critical temperature $T_{c}$ (defined by the condition $\frac{T_{c}-T}{T_{c}}\ll1$) and by the Werthamer–Helfand–Hohenberg (WHH) model across the entire temperature range. [44, 45]

The pressure within a diamond anvil cell can be determined using the $H_{2}$ (or $D_{2}$) vibron scale, which is based on the Raman spectroscopy of the hydrogen (or deuterium) medium contained within the cell. [54]
The pressure within a diamond anvil cell can be estimated by measuring the Raman shift of the high-frequency edge of the Raman line emitted by the stressed diamond anvil. [55]

reasoning.step: The authors synthesized yttrium hydrides in a diamond anvil cell (DAC) by compressing yttrium metal or $YH_{3}$ in a hydrogen ($H_2$) medium to high pressures (160–395 GPa) and heating with a pulsed laser.

Using X-ray powder diffraction, the authors identified a hexagonal close-packed (hcp) phase with space group $P6_3/mmc$ formed after laser heating at pressures above 185 GPa. The lattice parameters and volume-pressure dependence fitted with the Vinet equation of state [@premise-3] match theoretical predictions for $YH_{9}$ [@premise-1].
The composition of the hcp phase was independently estimated as $YH_{8.5(5)}$ by analyzing the hydrogen-induced volume expansion $V_{H}$, consistent with the assigned $YH_9$ stoichiometry.

Electrical resistance measurements were performed on Sample 2. Before laser heating, the sample contained bcc-$YH_{6}$ and showed a superconducting transition at $T_{c} \sim 211$ K at 201 GPa. After laser heating at 2000(10) K, the phase transformed to hcp-$YH_{9}$, and the superconducting transition shifted to a higher temperature of $T_{c} \sim 243$ K.

step.title: Verification of Superconductivity via Isotope Effect

reasoning.step: The authors substituted hydrogen with deuterium and observed a shift in $T_{c}$ from $\sim 243$ K (hydride) to $\sim 172$ K (deuteride) in the hcp phase. The calculated isotope coefficient $\alpha$ is 0.50 [@premise-4], supporting a conventional phonon-mediated mechanism.

step.title: Verification of Superconductivity via Magnetic Field

reasoning.step: The resistance drop was measured under external magnetic fields. $T_{c}$ decreased as the magnetic field increased. The upper critical field $H_{c2}(0K)$ was estimated using WHH and GL models [@premise-6], confirming the superconducting nature of the transition.
The authors measured $T_c$ for hcp-$YH_9$ at various pressures. The data reveal a "dome-like" shape with a maximum $T_c$ of 243 K at 201 GPa.

A sharp drop in $T_c$ was observed for $YH_9$ at pressures below $\sim 195$ GPa. This is attributed to structural instability, supported by the experimental observation that decreasing pressure in Sample 2 resulted in the decomposition of $YH_9$ into $bcc-YH_6$ at 159 GPa.

Despite theoretical predictions that fcc-$YH_{10}$ would have a higher $T_c$ ($>300$ K) [@premise-2], the authors could not synthesize this phase even at pressures up to 410 GPa and temperatures up to 2250 K.

Since $fcc-YH_{10}$ was not observed and the measured $T_c$ of $bcc-YH_6$ (max $\sim 224$ K) is lower than that of $hcp-YH_9$, the authors conclude that $hcp-YH_9$ with $T_c \sim 243$ K represents the highest critical temperature observed in the yttrium-hydrogen system in this study.

inference_unit.conclusion: The authors report the synthesis and discovery of superconductivity in the hexagonal close-packed (hcp) $P6_3/mmc$ phase of yttrium nonahydride ($YH_{9}$). This phase exhibits a maximum superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ of $\sim 243$ K at a pressure of 201 GPa, which represents the highest $T_{c}$ observed in the yttrium-hydrogen system in this study. The pressure dependence of $T_{c}$ forms a dome-like shape, with a sharp drop in $T_{c}$ below $\sim 195$ GPa attributed to structural instability.
